The file path is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:14 The preview (first 10000 characters) of the result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A Good read for Meat Eaters, and Veggie Heads as well", "text": "We live in an area where most of our neighbors hunt. Although we've been here for 20 years, I would say that it's only been within the past five years that this former city girl has embraced the country lifestyle. That lifestyle includes hunting, something that I've almost lost some city friends over.

My feelings about hunting are as follows...although I myself eat very little meat, my dogs are my family, and what can I say, it's a family of carnivores! In addition, when you are aware of the dark side of those pretty plastic wrapped packages of meat in the supermarket, hunting seems pretty darn humane, and a reasonable way to procure meat for your family. I think of what the cows and chickens made into dog kibble went through, and what diseases they might have had before being in a can of cat food.

That being said, I'm always interested in different perspectives about hunting, lifestyles, and philosophies.

I enjoyed this book quite a bit because it contains all of the above. There seem to be so many books about hunting, magazines about hunting. {"code": "import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Get file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']

# Load data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert rating to numeric
if 'rating' in df_reviews.columns:
    df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'], errors='coerce')

# Extract publication year from details
def extract_year(details_str):
    if not details_str or pd.isna(details_str):
        return None
    
    # Look for patterns like "released on January 1, 2004" or "published on May 18, 2014"
    patterns = [
        r'released on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'published on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'first edition on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'edition on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'released on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2} (\d{4})',
        r'published on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2} (\d{4})',
        r'published on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}(\d{4})'  # Without comma
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year_str = match.group(1)
            # Clean up the year
            if len(year_str) == 4 and year_str.isdigit():
                try:
                    year = int(year_str)
                    if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                        return year
                except:
                    continue
    
    # Alternative: look for any 4-digit year between 1900-2023
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_str)
    if year_matches:
        try:
            year = int(year_matches[0])
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
        except:
            pass
    
    return None

# Extract years and create decade column
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(
    lambda x: f"{x//10*10}s" if pd.notna(x) else None
)

# Link books to reviews - need to match book_id with purchase_id
# First, check if purchase_id follows a pattern we can map
print("Sample purchase_ids:")
print(df_reviews['purchase_id'].head(10).tolist())

print("\nSample book_ids:")
print(df_books['book_id'].head(10).tolist())

# Count books with valid years and reviews
print(f"\nBooks with valid years: {df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum()}")
print(f"Total books: {len(df_books)}")
print(f"Total reviews: {len(df_reviews)}")

# Check unique books in reviews
review_books = set(df_reviews['purchase_id'].unique())
book_ids = set(df_books['book_id'].unique())
print(f"Unique books in reviews: {len(review_books)}")
print(f"Unique books in catalog: {len(book_ids)}")

# Try to find books that have both year and reviews
# This is tricky - book_id and purchase_id might not match directly
# Let's check if there are any common patterns

# Look for purchase_ids that end with numbers that match book_ids
book_id_numbers = [int(b.split('_')[1]) for b in book_ids if b.startswith('bookid_')]
review_id_numbers = []
for pid in review_books:
    if '_' in pid:
        try:
            num = int(pid.split('_')[1])
            review_id_numbers.append(num)
        except:
            continue

print(f"Book ID number range: {min(book_id_numbers) if book_id_numbers else 'N/A'} to {max(book_id_numbers) if book_id_numbers else 'N/A'}")
print(f"Review purchase_id number range: {min(review_id_numbers) if review_id_numbers else 'N/A'} to {max(review_id_numbers) if review_id_numbers else 'N/A'}")"}

books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']

# Load data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert rating to numeric
if 'rating' in df_reviews.columns:
    df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'], errors='coerce')

# Extract publication year from details
def extract_year(details_str):
    if not details_str or pd.isna(details_str):
        return None
    
    # Look for patterns
    patterns = [
        r'released on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'published on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'first edition on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'edition on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year_str = match.group(1)
            if len(year_str) == 4 and year_str.isdigit():
                try:
                    year = int(year_str)
                    if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                        return year
                except:
                    continue
    
    # Alternative: look for any 4-digit year
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details_str)
    if year_matches:
        try:
            year = int(year_matches[0])
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
        except:
            pass
    
    return None

# Extract years and create decade column
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(
    lambda x: str(int(x//10*10)) + 's' if pd.notna(x) else None
)

# Check data
print('Sample purchase_ids:')
print(df_reviews['purchase_id'].head(5).tolist())

print('\nSample book_ids:')
print(df_books['book_id'].head(5).tolist())

print('\nBooks with valid years:')
print(df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum())
print(df_books['decade'].notna().sum())

# Create decade stats
if 'purchase_id' in df_reviews.columns and 'book_id' in df_books.columns:
    # Try to map purchase_id to book_id by the number after underscore
    # purchaseid_186 -> bookid_186
    def map_purchase_to_book(purchase_id):
        try:
            if '_' in purchase_id:
                num = purchase_id.split('_')[1]
                return 'bookid_' + num
        except:
            pass
        return None
    
    df_reviews['mapped_book_id'] = df_reviews['purchase_id'].apply(map_purchase_to_book)
    
    # Count reviews per mapped book
    review_counts = df_reviews.groupby('mapped_book_id').size()
    books_with_reviews = review_counts[review_counts >= 10].index.tolist()
    
    # Filter books that have publication year and at least 10 reviews
    df_filtered = df_books[
        (df_books['publication_year'].notna()) & 
        (df_books['book_id'].isin(books_with_reviews))
    ]
    
    # Add ratings to filtered books
    book_ratings = df_reviews.groupby('mapped_book_id')['rating'].mean()
    df_filtered['avg_rating'] = df_filtered['book_id'].map(book_ratings)
    
    # Group by decade

import re
import pandas as pd

# Load the data
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:34']

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert rating to numeric
df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'], errors='coerce')

# Extract publication year

def extract_year(details):
    if pd.isna(details) or not details:
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns
    patterns = [
        r'released on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'published on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'first edition on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})',
        r'edition on [A-Za-z]+ \d{1,2}, (\d{4})'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    
    # Fallback: find any 4-digit year
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
    if years:
        year = int(years[0])
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract publication years and create decade column
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(
    lambda x: f"{int(x//10*10)}s" if pd.notna(x) else None
)

print(f"Books with valid years: {df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum()} out of {len(df_books)}")

# Extract numeric IDs
def get_numeric_id(id_str):
    try:
        if isinstance(id_str, str) and '_' in id_str:
            return id_str.split('_')[1]
    except:
        pass
    return None

df_books['book_num'] = df_books['book_id'].apply(get_numeric_id)
df_reviews['review_num'] = df_reviews['purchase_id'].apply(get_numeric_id)

# Merge books and reviews on numeric IDs
df_merged = df_books.merge(df_reviews, left_on='book_num', right_on='review_num', how='inner')

print(f"Successfully merged records: {len(df_merged)}")

# Group by book and calculate stats
book_stats = df_merged.groupby(['book_id', 'decade']).agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count'],
    'publication_year': 'first'
}).reset_index()

# Flatten column names
book_stats.columns = ['book_id', 'decade', 'avg_rating', 'review_count', 'publication_year']

print(f"Books with reviews: {len(book_stats)}")

# Filter books with at least 10 reviews
books_with_enough_reviews = book_stats[book_stats['review_count'] >= 10]

print(f"Books with >=10 reviews: {len(books_with_enough_reviews)}")

# Group by decade and calculate stats
decade_stats = books_with_enough_reviews.groupby('decade').agg({
    'book_id': 'count',
    'avg_rating': 'mean'
}).rename(columns={'book_id': 'book_count'})

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['book_count'] >= 10]

print(f"Decades with >=10 books (each with >=10 reviews): {len(decade_stats_filtered)}")
print("\nDecade stats:")
print(decade_stats_filtered)

# Find best decade
if not decade_stats_filtered.empty:
    best_decade = decade_stats_filtered['avg_rating'].idxmax()
    best_rating = decade_stats_filtered.loc[best_decade, 'avg_rating']
    book_count = decade_stats_filtered.loc[best_decade, 'book_count']
    
    result = {
        'best_decade': str(best_decade),
        'avg_rating': round(float(best_rating), 3),
        'book_count': int(book_count)
    }
else:
    result = {'error': 'No decade meets the criteria'}

print('\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}
